name: Red's Barbecued Brisket
recipeInstructions:
1. Select "packer trimmed" briskets in the ten to twelve pound category for barbecue in Texas. Avoid closely trimmed or "value packed" briskets, as they lack fat that aids in tenderness.
2. Choose the brisket with the smallest fat kernel on one side and squeeze it with both thumbs.
3. Build your fire using a mixture of mesquite and oak. Rub the brisket thoroughly with Red's Dry Rub, ensuring all surfaces are coated to seal the meat and add flavor.
4. Coat the entire brisket with lemon juice and rub in well. Sprinkle the dry rub generously over the brisket, rubbing it in well to ensure complete coverage.
5. Place the brisket away from direct heat on one side of the pit, fat side up, allowing gravity and nature to baste it. Avoid touching the meat for 12 hours.
6. Keep "helpers" (guests) occupied by cooking pork ribs with leftover rub and basting them in the hotter end of the pit.
7. After 12 hours, generously slather the brisket with Red's Basting Sauce, wrap it tightly in aluminum foil, and return it to the pit.
8. Seal off all air supplies to the fire and allow the meat to "set" for three or four hours, which tenderizes the meat.
9. Serve the brisket with beans, cole slaw, Jalepenos, onions, pickles, and plenty of bread. Accompany it with cold beer or iced tea.
A ten-pound brisket will yield about 8-16 servings, depending on the individual brisket and guest appetites.
